name: vault
context: Encrypt, view, rekey, and inject Ansible secrets without leaving plaintext in the repository
info: Ansible Vault is the built-in secret-handling surface for playbooks and inventory: it encrypts structured vars files, entire task files, or individual secret strings while preserving Git-friendly workflows. Agents use this skill when they need to protect production credentials, rekey files between environments, encrypt inline variables for `group_vars` or `host_vars`, and execute playbooks with the correct vault identity. The operational hazards are leaking password files, printing secrets to stdout, and mixing vault IDs incorrectly, so this node emphasizes file hygiene and run-time secret injection rather than casual encryption.
triggers: encrypt a vars file with ansible vault
encrypt a string for group vars
run a playbook with vault id
keep ansible secrets out of git
body:
# Ansible Vault

Vault protects secret material while keeping the rest of your project readable in Git. Use it for credentials, API tokens, passwords, certificates, and other values that [[ansible/playbooks]] or [[ansible/inventory]] must consume at runtime.

## File Operations

```bash
ansible-vault encrypt group_vars/prod/secrets.yml --vault-id prod@prompt
ansible-vault view group_vars/prod/secrets.yml --vault-id prod@~/.ansible/prod.vault
ansible-vault rekey group_vars/prod/secrets.yml --vault-id prod@prompt --new-vault-id prod@~/.ansible/new-prod.vault
```

## Inline Secret Example

```bash
ansible-vault encrypt_string --vault-id dev@prompt --name db_password supersecret
```

The output can be pasted into `group_vars`, `host_vars`, or role vars files without exposing the plaintext value.

## Playbook Integration

```bash
ansible-playbook -i inventory site.yml --vault-id prod@prompt
ansible-playbook -i inventory site.yml --vault-password-file ~/.ansible/prod.vault
```

## Secret Hygiene

- Keep vault password files out of Git and out of shared temp directories.
- Use different vault IDs for different environments when separation matters.
- Apply `no_log: true` to tasks that might echo sensitive values.
- Prefer encrypted vars files to copying secrets into shell history or CI logs.

sections.title: encrypt, decrypt, view, and rekey files
sections.body: Use `ansible-vault encrypt` to protect an existing file, `view` to inspect it without leaving a decrypted copy on disk, and `rekey` to rotate secrets or move a file to a new vault identity. Rekeying is a normal maintenance task for environment rotation and offboarding. When multiple vault IDs are present, specify the encryption identity explicitly.

sections.title: encrypt_string for inline variables
sections.body: Use `ansible-vault encrypt_string --name secret_name value` when you only need to protect one or two values inside an otherwise readable vars file. This keeps the surrounding YAML understandable while still storing ciphertext in Git. It is often the least disruptive way to add one secret to an existing project.

sections.title: vault-id lets one repo carry multiple secret domains
sections.body: Vault IDs let you separate secret material by environment or trust boundary, for example `dev`, `staging`, and `prod`. Pass `--vault-id env@prompt` or `--vault-id env@/path/to/file` so Ansible knows which secret to use. This becomes important when one repository carries playbooks that can target more than one environment.

sections.title: Keep secrets out of logs and history
sections.body: Even when the source file is encrypted, secrets can leak during execution if a task prints them or if a password file is handled carelessly. Use `no_log: true` on sensitive tasks, store password files in protected locations, and avoid exposing secret values through shell history, CI annotations, or debug output.

scripts.name: Manage Vault Secrets
scripts.description: Encrypt, view, rekey, decrypt, or encrypt a string with ansible-vault

ANSIBLE_VAULT_ACTION.description: Vault action: encrypt, decrypt, view, edit, create, encrypt-string, or rekey

ANSIBLE_VAULT_PASSWORD_FILE.description: Path to the current vault password file

ANSIBLE_VAULT_ID.description: Vault identity such as prod@prompt or dev@/path/to/file

ANSIBLE_VAULT_NEW_ID.description: New vault identity used during rekey

ANSIBLE_VAULT_NEW_PASSWORD_FILE.description: New vault password file used during rekey

target.description: Path to the target file for file-based actions

vaultId.description: Vault identity such as prod@prompt or dev@file

passwordFile.description: Path to the current vault password file

newVaultId.description: New vault identity used when rekeying

command.description: The ansible-vault command that was executed
